children.name: Oryctolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the European rabbit, ancestor of all domestic rabbit breeds and a keystone species whose burrowing and grazing profoundly shape the ecology of Mediterranean scrublands and grasslands.

children.name: Lepus
children.description: The true hares, the largest genus of Leporidae with around 32 species adapted to open habitats from Arctic tundra to desert, characterised by longer legs, larger ears, and a preference for running over burrowing.

children.name: Lepus timidus
children.description: Category:Mammals described in 1758 Category:Animal taxa named by Carl Linnaeus.

children.name: Lepus californicus
children.description: Category:Hares Category:Fauna of the California chaparral and woodlands.

children.name: Lepus connectens
children.description: The Abyssinian hare is found in the highlands of Ethiopia, typically in open grassland and heathland habitats at elevations above 1,800 metres. It is closely related to the Cape hare and has historically been treated as a subspecies of it. Its relatively restricted range in the Ethiopian Highlands makes it potentially susceptible to habitat degradation and agricultural encroachment.

children.name: Lepus fagani
children.description: #REDIRECT Ethiopian hare Category:Mammals described in 1903.

children.name: Lepus meiguensis
children.description: The Meigu hare is a recently described species known from a restricted area of Sichuan Province in southwestern China, discovered through morphological and molecular analyses. It inhabits alpine meadows and shrubland at high elevations in the Daxue Mountains. It is considered Data Deficient by the IUCN and requires further survey work to determine its true range and conservation status.

children.name: Lepus baicalensis
children.description: The Baikal hare is distributed across the forested and shrubby margins of Lake Baikal and the broader steppe and forest-steppe zone of Transbaikalia in Russia and adjacent Mongolia. It occupies a transitional zone between the forested mountain hare and the open-steppe Tolai hare and has sometimes been treated as a subspecies of the latter. It is characterised by a patchy seasonal moult and mixed forested-steppe ecology.

children.name: Lepus whitakeri
children.description: Whitaker's hare is a poorly known species of North Africa, restricted to coastal areas of Tunisia, Libya and possibly adjacent Algeria. It inhabits sandy coastal plains, dunes and low scrub near the Mediterranean coast. Long treated as a subspecies of the Cape hare, genetic and morphological evidence supports its recognition as a full species.

children.name: Lepus halli
children.description: Hall's hare was described from specimens collected in the grasslands and shrublands of central Mexico. It is closely related to the black-tailed jackrabbit and has at times been treated as its subspecies. Its taxonomy remains under active review and it is classified as Data Deficient by the IUCN pending further morphological and genetic analysis.

children.name: Lepus oaxacensis
children.description: The Oaxacan jackrabbit is restricted to dry grassland, scrub and open savanna in the Tehuantepec region and Pacific slopes of Oaxaca and Chiapas, Mexico. It is closely related to the black-tailed jackrabbit and distinguished by subtle morphological and genetic characters. Its restricted range in an area of rapid agricultural change makes it a species of conservation concern.

children.name: Lepus kunmingensis
children.description: The Kunming hare is known from the Kunming Basin and adjacent highlands of Yunnan Province, China, where it inhabits cultivated fields, grasslands and open shrubland at middle elevations. It was described from specimens collected around Kunming city. It is closely related to, and sometimes confused with, the Yunnan hare Lepus comus, and its taxonomic boundaries are still being clarified.

children.name: Lepus butleri
children.description: The Uganda hare is a little-known species from the savanna and woodland zones of Uganda and adjacent parts of South Sudan and the Democratic Republic of Congo. Described on the basis of limited museum specimens, it has been variously synonymised with the African savanna hare or Scrub hare before being maintained as a distinct species by recent authorities. Field surveys of its ecology and distribution remain sparse.

children.name: Lepus whytei
children.description: Whyte's hare is recorded from the Nyika Plateau and montane grasslands of Malawi and adjacent Zambia, inhabiting high-altitude grassland and heathland above 1,800 metres. It is a medium-sized, grizzled-brown hare that has been shuffled between species and subspecies status within the African hare complex. Its montane distribution suggests it is likely reproductively isolated from the lowland populations of related species.

children.name: Lepus starki
children.description: Stark's hare is a little-known African species from the savanna and semi-arid zones of Kenya and northern Tanzania, closely allied to the Cape hare. It inhabits open dry grassland and Acacia bushland. Its taxonomic identity has been debated and it has sometimes been treated as a synonym of Lepus capensis, though genetic evidence suggests it is a distinct lineage.

children.name: Lepus zulu
children.description: The Natal scrub hare is a recently recognised species from the eastern seaboard of South Africa, separated from the main scrub hare on the basis of morphological and molecular differences. It inhabits dense coastal thicket, shrubby vegetation and the margins of forest in KwaZulu-Natal and Mpumalanga. Its relationship to the broader Lepus saxatilis complex continues to be refined as genetic data accumulates.

children.name: Lepus somalicus
children.description: The Somali hare is found in the arid plains, semi-desert and dry thorn scrub of Somalia, Djibouti and the Ogaden region of Ethiopia. It is a pale, large-eared hare well adapted to hot, arid conditions. Its taxonomic status vis-à-vis the Cape hare and Abyssinian hare has been disputed, but recent morphological work supports its recognition as a distinct species.

children.name: Lepus crawshayi
children.description: #REDIRECT African savanna hare {{Redirect category shell| }}.

children.name: Lepus kabylicus
children.description: The Algerian hare is endemic to northern Algeria, inhabiting the Atlas Mountains and adjacent coastal ranges in scrubland, garrigue and open forest. It was described in the 19th century and was long subsumed within the Cape hare, but is now treated as a distinct Maghrebian species. It faces increasing pressure from agricultural development and hunting in its restricted range.

children.name: Lepus aegyptius
children.description: The Egyptian hare is a widespread species of the Nile Valley, delta and adjacent semi-desert regions of Egypt and the Sinai Peninsula, extending into parts of Sudan and Libya. It is a medium-sized, pale sandy hare adapted to hot and arid lowland conditions. It has historically been viewed as a subspecies of the Cape hare, but is now recognised as distinct based on its distinctive morphology and genetics.

children.name: Lepus arabicus
children.description: The Arabian hare is native to the Arabian Peninsula including Saudi Arabia, Oman, Yemen and the United Arab Emirates, inhabiting gravel desert, sandy plains and scrub at low to mid elevations. It is a small, pale hare with large ears for heat dissipation in the extreme heat of the Arabian Desert. Its taxonomy relative to the Cape hare is still debated, with some authorities treating it as a subspecies of Lepus capensis.

children.name: Lepus omanensis
children.description: The Omani hare is a small to medium-sized hare endemic to the mountains and adjacent plains of Oman and the UAE, inhabiting wadis, rocky hillsides and shrubby desert. It has been recognised as distinct from the Arabian hare on the basis of its smaller size and isolated montane distribution in the Hajar Mountains. It is assessed as Least Concern by the IUCN but remains poorly surveyed.

children.name: Lepus nicodemus
children.description: Nicodemus's hare is a little-known species from the Great Lakes region of east-central Africa, recorded from Rwanda, Burundi and adjacent DRC. It inhabits highland grasslands, moorlands and agricultural margins at elevations above 1,500 metres. Its taxonomic distinctiveness from adjacent species in the Cape hare complex has been upheld by recent genetic analyses.

children.name: Lepus pestifer
children.description: Lepus pestifer is a hare of the high-altitude meadows and alpine shrublands of the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau transitional zone in western China, where it can reach very high densities in productive grassland. It has been treated as a subspecies of the woolly hare or Tibetan hare but is recognised as a distinct species by Chinese mammalogists. Its population dynamics and habitat requirements on the Plateau remain under investigation.

children.name: Lepus dayanus
children.description: The Pakistan hare inhabits the dry scrubland, stony plains and agricultural areas of Pakistan, northwestern India and eastern Afghanistan. Long treated as a subspecies of the Indian hare Lepus nigricollis, it differs in pelage colour, morphometry and ecology and is recognised as a distinct species by recent authorities. It is the most common large mammal in many arid agricultural landscapes of Pakistan.

children.name: Lepus ferghanensis
children.description: The Ferghana hare is native to the Ferghana Valley and adjacent mountain foothills of Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan, where it inhabits the scrubby steppe and lower mountain slopes of this densely populated Central Asian valley. It is closely related to the Tolai hare and has been treated as its subspecies, but morphological and distributional evidence supports species-level recognition. It faces pressure from intensive agriculture in the Ferghana Valley.

children.name: Lepus pamirensis
children.description: The Pamir hare inhabits the cold high-altitude desert and alpine meadow zone of the Pamir and Karakoram ranges in Tajikistan, Afghanistan and northern Pakistan above 3,500 metres. It has generally been treated as a subspecies of the desert hare Lepus tibetanus but is geographically isolated and morphologically distinct. Its small range and high-altitude specialisation make it potentially vulnerable to climate change.

children.name: Lepus ochropus
children.description: The ochre-footed hare is recorded from the wooded grasslands and miombo of Tanzania and northern Mozambique, characterised by its pale ochre-coloured legs against a greyer body. It has been variably placed as a subspecies of the savanna hare or as a distinct species, with most modern treatments accepting its specific distinctness. It is patchily distributed and little studied in the field.

children.name: Lepus isabelinus
children.description: The Persian hare is distributed across the semi-arid plateaux, stony plains and shrubby steppes of Iran, Afghanistan and adjacent Turkmenistan. It is a medium-to-large pale hare with a sandy-buff dorsum and white undersides, blending well with the arid substrates of the Iranian Plateau. It is assessed as Least Concern by the IUCN and is relatively common in appropriate steppe habitats.

children.name: Lepus keniensis
children.description: The Kenya hare is restricted to the highland grasslands and montane heaths of the Kenyan Highlands and the Aberdare Mountains, at elevations generally above 2,000 metres. It is a medium-sized hare with grizzled brown-grey fur and relatively large ears. It has been treated as a subspecies of the African savanna hare but is recognised as distinct based on its ecology and montane isolation.

children.name: Sylvilagus
children.description: The cottontail rabbits, around 19 species distributed across the Americas from Alaska to Argentina, named for their distinctive fluffy white tail and occupying a wide range of habitats from desert to swamp.

children.name: Sylvilagus floridanus
children.description: #REDIRECT Eastern cottontail {{Redirect category shell| }}.

children.name: Sylvilagus cognatus
children.description: #REDIRECT Robust cottontail#%22Manzano Mountain cottontail%22.

children.name: Sylvilagus hirsutus
children.description: The Omiltemi cottontail is an extremely range-restricted species known only from the cloud forest of the Sierra Madre del Sur near Omiltemi in Guerrero, Mexico. It is assessed as Endangered by the IUCN owing to its tiny known range, ongoing deforestation and the general rarity of cloud forest in this part of Mexico. It was described in 1904 and very few specimens have been collected since.

children.name: Sylvilagus veraecrucis
children.description: The Veracruz cottontail is a poorly known species endemic to the humid tropical lowlands of Veracruz state on Mexico's Gulf coast. It inhabits dense tropical scrub and the margins of tropical forests in an area subject to extensive agricultural development. It is assessed as Near Threatened by the IUCN given ongoing habitat loss in the lowland tropics of eastern Mexico.

children.name: Pentalagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Amami rabbit, a primitive and endangered species endemic to two Japanese islands, notable for its small eyes, short ears, and dark pelage — a 'living fossil' among leporids.

children.name: Nesolagus
children.description: The striped rabbits, a small genus of two species from Southeast Asia characterised by striking dark-striped pelage — among the most visually distinctive of all leporids.

children.name: Romerolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the volcano rabbit, the world's second smallest rabbit, endemic to four volcanoes near Mexico City and representing one of the most geographically restricted mammals in North America.

children.name: Romerolagus diazi
children.description: Category:Taxa named by Fernando Ferrari-Pérez.

children.name: Caprolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the hispid hare, a highly threatened species from the South Asian foothills characterised by coarse, bristly fur unlike any other leporid.

children.name: Poelagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the Bunyoro rabbit, a central African species that fills an ecological niche similar to European rabbits in the savanna-forest mosaic of equatorial Africa.

children.name: Pronolagus
children.description: The red rock hares, four species endemic to southern and eastern Africa, all characterised by rufous-red pelage and a close association with rocky habitat.

children.name: Brachylagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the pygmy rabbit, the smallest leporid in North America, uniquely associated with tall sagebrush in the Great Basin.

children.name: Brachylagus idahoensis
children.description: The pygmy rabbit is a rabbit species native to the United States. It is one of the only two native rabbit species in North America to dig its own burrow. The pygmy rabbit differs significantly from species within either the Lepus (hare) or Sylvilagus (cottontail) genera, and was once considered to be within the genus Brachylagus, which includes one extinct species.

children.name: Bunolagus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the riverine rabbit, one of the world's most critically endangered mammals, restricted to the central Karoo of South Africa.

children.name: Bunolagus monticularis
children.description: The riverine rabbit, also known as the bushman rabbit or bushman hare, is a species of rabbit that lives among patches of thick vegetation in the Karoo of South Africa's Western and Northern Cape provinces. It is the only member of the genus Bunolagus. It is classified a critically endangered species by the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN); the most recent estimates of the species' population range from 157 to 207 mature individuals, and 224 to 380 total.
